purpose: Draft (inactive) question objects derived from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch -- a completed, handwritten 'Review Quiz #1' (20 fill-in-blank questions) plus a liferaft parts-labeling worksheet. These are NOT part of src/content/course.json's live "questions" array and will not appear in the app until explicitly promoted, after each question's verificationStatus is upgraded from draft-unverified and each fact is checked against Vinci's confirmed manual copy (per the source intake's needsVerification/verificationStatus fields, which are unverified on every source item).
activationRule: Do not add any of these questions to course.json unless: (1) its verificationStatus has been changed away from 'draft-unverified' following direct confirmation against the manual, and (2) for the 5 image-based questions, the shared liferaft-diagram photo (2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg) has been cropped to remove the pencil doodles/scribbles overlaid on the raw photo -- see public/content/vinci/2026-07-08-quiz-review-1/README.md.
duplicateCheckNotes: Cross-referenced against all 145 live questions in src/content/course.json (not just chapter-2/chapter-3) before drafting. Skipped as clear duplicates: Q1 'What does the Station Bill list?' (duplicates q-ch1-vocab-stationbill-easy), Q3 'abandon ship drill passenger actions' (the 'report to muster station' half duplicates a choice already in q-ch1-abandonship-drill-components-medium), Q6 'limber holes' (duplicates q-gloss-limber), and Q10 'close the drain before launch' (duplicates q-ch2-boatplug / q-ch2-drainplugfirst-hard). Q16 ('Oars' command alone) was folded into the combined rowing-commands question (q-quizr1-rowing-commands-compare-vh) rather than drafted separately, since testing it standalone would have restated a fact already covered there. Chapter 3 was prioritized per instructions: of the 6 fresh liferaft facts drafted, all were deliberately chosen to AVOID the liferaft topics the live bank already covers heavily (righting a capsized raft / CO2-bottle-side technique -- tested 4 ways already by q-raft-1/2/7/11; ballast-bag purpose alone -- q-raft-9; canopy-alone benefit -- q-raft-5; inflation-trigger mechanism -- q-raft-3), instead drafting parts of the diagram with zero existing coverage (weak link, sea anchor, inflatable floor, internal/external lifelines, recognition light, water-activated battery, floating sheath knife). Diagram item #15 (left blank by the student, no visible numbered arrow) and the 'PRV' vs. 'Pressure pellet valve' label discrepancy on item #4 were both excluded entirely rather than guessed, per instructions.

questions.prompt: In open lifeboats, how is the releasing lever specifically labeled and marked?

choices.text: Painted red, labeled 'Danger -- lever drops boat', with a 12-inch-wide white stripe
Painted the same international orange as the rest of the boat, with no special labeling
choices.misconception: Misses that the lever itself gets distinct red/white marking, separate from the boat's general international-orange paint.

choices.text: Painted green with a 'Caution' label only
choices.misconception: Wrong color, and understates the warning wording actually used.

choices.text: Left unpainted, relying on a placard posted elsewhere on the boat
choices.misconception: Misses that the lever itself is directly painted and labeled, not just referenced by a separate placard.

hints: The lever's own color is a strong contrast to the boat's general orange scheme, on purpose.
The wording isn't a vague 'caution' -- it spells out exactly what pulling the lever does.
questions.explanation: In open lifeboats, the releasing lever is painted red and labeled 'Danger -- lever drops boat', with a 12-inch-wide white stripe -- a deliberately unmistakable marking distinct from the boat's general international-orange paint scheme.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-4.jpg (Review Quiz #1, question 14 of 20); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A liferaft's painter line normally parts on its own at a built-in weak link under excess strain. Why is a floating sheath knife also kept near the raft's entrance?

choices.text: As a backup -- in case the weak link fails to part, the line can be cut manually to free the raft
To repair tears in the canopy fabric
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated repair function for the knife.

choices.text: To open food ration packaging only
choices.misconception: Understates the knife's actual safety purpose.

choices.text: To signal rescuers by reflecting light off the blade
choices.misconception: Confuses a cutting tool with an unrelated signaling function.

hints: The weak link is meant to fail automatically, but a backup plan exists for when it doesn't.
The knife floats on purpose -- so it isn't lost overboard if dropped while cutting a line at the raft's entrance.
questions.explanation: A liferaft's painter is designed to part at a built-in weak link under excess strain, so the raft isn't dragged under with a sinking ship. The floating sheath knife kept near the entrance is a manual backup for exactly the case where the weak link fails to part -- the line can be cut by hand instead.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 17 'Weak link' and 23 'Floating Sheath Knife'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A liferaft's rigging includes both a 'weak link' and a towing bridle/connection. What's the key difference in how each is designed to behave under strain?

choices.text: The weak link is deliberately designed to part (break) under excess strain, so the raft isn't dragged under with a sinking ship; the towing bridle/connection is designed to hold under load, for actually towing the raft
Both are designed to hold under any amount of strain, with no intentional failure point anywhere in the system
choices.misconception: Misses that the weak link is intentionally the system's failure point -- that's its entire purpose.

choices.text: The towing bridle is the part designed to break; the weak link is what holds during towing
choices.misconception: Reverses the two components' actual roles.

choices.text: They're the same fitting under two different names
choices.misconception: Treats two distinct, separately labeled fittings as if they were one.

hints: One of these two fittings is supposed to fail on purpose -- the other is supposed to never fail.
Think about what would happen to the raft if the ship it's still attached to starts sinking fast.
questions.explanation: A weak link is deliberately built into a liferaft's painter/towing line so it parts under excess strain -- protecting the raft from being dragged under by a sinking ship. The towing bridle and towing connection, by contrast, are the load-bearing hardware meant to hold firmly while a raft is actually being towed by a rescue vessel.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 17 'Weak link', 19 'Towing Connection', and 20 'Towing bridle'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch

questions.prompt: A liferaft has both a sea anchor and ballast bags. What's the difference in what each one does?

choices.text: The sea anchor (deployed on the towing/painter line) stabilizes the raft's drift and slows it; the ballast bags (hanging beneath the raft in the water) add stability and resist capsizing by weighting the raft's underside
Both serve the exact same anti-capsize function, just mounted in different places
choices.misconception: Collapses two distinct functions -- drift stabilization vs. anti-capsize weighting -- into one.

choices.text: The ballast bags stabilize drift, and the sea anchor resists capsizing
choices.misconception: Swaps the two fittings' actual functions.

choices.text: The sea anchor is a fishing tool, unrelated to raft stability
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated function for the sea anchor.

hints: One of these fittings is about controlling which way and how fast the raft drifts; the other is about keeping the raft from flipping over.
questions.explanation: A sea anchor (or drogue) is deployed on the raft's towing/painter line to stabilize its drift and slow unwanted movement across the water. Ballast bags are separate water pockets that hang beneath the raft, adding stability and resisting capsizing by weighting the raft's underside -- two different fittings solving two different problems.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 13 'Ballast bags' and 18 'Sea Anchor'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch

questions.prompt: A liferaft has both a canopy and an inflatable floor. Both help protect survivors from the cold, but from which direction?

choices.text: The canopy protects from wind, spray, and heat loss from above and the sides; the inflatable floor insulates against cold transmitted from the water directly beneath the raft
Both protect against cold from the same direction -- overhead only
choices.misconception: Misses that the floor addresses a completely different exposure direction (from below) than the canopy (from above/the sides).

choices.text: The inflatable floor is only for buoyancy, not insulation
choices.misconception: Drops the floor's real insulating role against cold transmitted from the water below.

choices.text: Only the canopy matters for cold protection; the floor's shape is irrelevant to warmth
choices.misconception: Dismisses the floor's genuine insulating function.

hints: Cold can reach survivors from more than one direction in a liferaft -- wind and spray from the sides/above, and conducted cold from the water right underneath them.
questions.explanation: The canopy protects occupants from wind, spray, and heat loss from above and the sides, while the inflatable floor insulates against cold conducted up from the water directly beneath the raft -- two separate features addressing cold exposure from two different directions.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 6 'Canopy' and 22 'Inflatable floor'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch

questions.prompt: A liferaft has both an internal lifeline and external lifelines. What's the difference?

choices.text: The internal lifeline runs inside the raft for occupants to hold onto; the external lifelines run around the outside for swimmers in the water to grab onto
They're the same line, just referred to differently depending on which side you're looking from
choices.misconception: Treats two separately labeled fittings, mounted in two different locations, as if they were one.

choices.text: The internal lifeline is for swimmers in the water; the external lifelines are for occupants inside the raft
choices.misconception: Swaps the two fittings' actual locations and purposes.

choices.text: Only the external lifelines actually exist on a liferaft; 'internal lifeline' just means the same fitting
choices.misconception: Denies that the raft has a genuinely separate interior fitting.

hints: One of these is for people already inside the raft; the other is for people still in the water.
questions.explanation: The internal lifeline runs inside the raft (across the canopy interior) for occupants to hold onto, while the external lifelines run around the outside of the raft for swimmers still in the water to grab onto.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 1 'Internal lifeline' and 12 'External lifelines'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch

questions.prompt: A liferaft has both a recognition light and a water-activated battery. How do these two features relate to each other?

choices.text: The water-activated battery automatically powers the recognition light once the raft is in the water, without anyone needing to manually turn it on
They're unrelated -- the recognition light runs on hand-cranked power, not any battery
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated power source for the recognition light.

choices.text: The water-activated battery is only a backup for a separate EPIRB, unrelated to the recognition light
choices.misconception: Invents a connection to unrelated equipment instead of the actual recognition-light link.

choices.text: The recognition light must be switched on manually, and the water-activated battery only powers the CO2 inflation system
choices.misconception: Misassigns what the water-activated battery actually powers, and drops the light's automatic activation.

hints: Think about why a light on a liferaft would need to work automatically, in the dark, without anyone fumbling for a switch.
questions.explanation: A liferaft's water-activated battery automatically powers its recognition light as soon as the raft is in the water -- no manual switch required, which matters since survivors may board in darkness or under stress.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-review-1, image 2026-07-07_063330-1.jpg (liferaft parts diagram, items 3 'Recognition light' and 7 'Water activated battery'); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-review-1-source-intake.json
questions.imageAlt: Photo of a hand-labeled liferaft parts diagram from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-review-1 batch

promotionHistory.promotedTo: src/content/course.json (verificationStatus set to quiz-derived-study, per the study-app status model)
promotionHistory.note: First promotion wave for this batch, per src/content/imports/vinci-filtered-question-recovery-inventory.md's 'quick wins' list. The remaining 7 questions below stay in this file, inactive: q-quizr1-releasing-lever-label and q-quizr1-liferaft-sheathknife need verification/revision; the five liferaft-diagram image questions (q-quizr1-weaklink-vs-towingbridle-vh, q-quizr1-seaanchor-vs-ballastbags, q-quizr1-floor-vs-canopy, q-quizr1-internal-vs-external-lifelines, q-quizr1-recognitionlight-waterbattery) are blocked on cropping the shared doodle-covered diagram photo.
